title: Pfizer — Vaccines — knowledge doc rsv
html:
Deep-Dive Market Context: RSV and Abrysvo

Product: Abrysvo (bivalent RSV prefusion F protein vaccine) Manufacturer: Pfizer Inc. Approved indications: Adults 60+ (May 2023), maternal immunization 32-36 weeks gestation (August 2023), adults 18-59 at increased risk (2024/2025) Document scope: Comprehensive RSV disease landscape, product profile, competitive dynamics, ACIP evolution, commercial trajectory, ZoomRx insights Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 RSV Virology

Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a negative-sense, single-stranded RNA virus of the family Pneumoviridae. It is an enveloped virus approximately 120-300 nm in diameter with two major surface glycoproteins that are critical to its pathogenic cycle:

  • Fusion (F) protein: Mediates fusion of the viral envelope with the host cell membrane. Exists in two conformations: a metastable prefusion (preF) state (lollipop-shaped, present on the virus surface before cell contact) and a stable postfusion (postF) state (crutch-shaped, after membrane fusion is complete). The prefusion conformation is the primary target for modern vaccine development because it exposes neutralization-sensitive epitopes (particularly antigenic site zero) that are hidden once the protein shifts to its postfusion form.
  • Attachment (G) protein: Mediates initial viral attachment to host cell surface glycosaminoglycans. More variable between subtypes than the F protein.

1.2 RSV A vs RSV B Subtypes

RSV circulates as two antigenic subtypes, RSV A and RSV B, which co-circulate during each seasonal epidemic with one typically predominating in a given year:

Feature RSV A RSV B
G protein Highly variable between subtypes (~47% amino acid identity) Distinct antigenic profile
F protein Highly conserved (>90% amino acid identity across subtypes) Highly conserved
Virulence Historically associated with greater disease severity in some studies Comparable severity in recent data
Predominance Alternates yearly; more frequently dominant in US seasons Less frequently dominant
Vaccine relevance Both subtypes must be covered for a complete vaccine Abrysvo's bivalent design addresses both

The critical insight for vaccine design: because the F protein is >90% conserved between RSV A and RSV B, prefusion F-based vaccines (including Abrysvo) can provide cross-subtype protection. Abrysvo's bivalent formulation includes stabilized preF antigens from both RSV A and RSV B lineages, and the RENOIR trial confirmed consistent efficacy against both subtypes across two RSV seasons.

1.3 Pathogenesis

RSV infects the respiratory epithelium via the nasopharynx and spreads to the lower respiratory tract through cell-to-cell fusion (forming syncytia — multinucleated giant cells — hence the virus name). The disease process involves:

  1. Viral entry: PreF protein undergoes a triggered conformational change, inserting its fusion peptide into the host cell membrane, forming a six-helix bundle that pulls viral and cell membranes together
  2. Epithelial damage: Direct cytopathic effect on airway epithelial cells, causing necrosis, sloughing, and mucus plugging
  3. Inflammatory response: Neutrophilic infiltration, cytokine release (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-alpha), airway edema and bronchospasm
  4. Resolution or progression: In healthy adults, self-limited upper respiratory illness. In high-risk populations, progression to bronchiolitis (infants), pneumonia, respiratory failure, and death

RSV does not confer durable natural immunity — reinfection occurs throughout life, though disease severity generally decreases with repeated exposures (except in the elderly and immunocompromised, where waning immunity restores susceptibility to severe disease).

1.4 Epidemiology — US Burden

RSV is one of the most significant respiratory pathogens in the US, with disease burden concentrated at the extremes of age:

Overall (2024-2025 season):

  • 190,000-350,000 hospitalizations annually
  • 10,000-23,000 deaths annually
  • RSV epidemic period: November 9, 2024 through March 29, 2025
  • RSV positivity peaked at 11.0% during the week ending December 21, 2024

Older adults (60+):

  • 60,000-160,000 hospitalizations/year among adults 65+
  • 6,000-10,000 deaths/year among adults 65+
  • 82.1% of RSV deaths occur in adults aged 65+
  • Highest hospitalization rates among adults 75+ (426.9 per 100,000)
  • Annual economic burden: $6.6 billion for adults 60+ ($2.9B direct medical, $3.7B indirect costs)
  • Only 4% of RSV cases in this population are hospitalized, but these account for 94% of direct medical costs

Infants and young children:

  • RSV is the leading cause of infant hospitalization in the US
  • ~58,000-80,000 hospitalizations/year in children under 5
  • Among 3.7 million US births annually: ~592,700 RSV-associated LRTI cases, 48,499 hospitalizations, 144,599 ED visits
  • Annual cost: $1.6 billion (infants 0-3 months account for 43% of costs)
  • Mean hospitalization cost: ~$12,000 per admission

Adults 50-59 at increased risk:

  • 15,000-20,000 RSV-associated hospitalizations annually in this age group
  • Newly eligible for vaccination following the April 2025 ACIP expansion

1.5 Seasonality

RSV follows a predictable seasonal pattern in the continental US:

  • Season onset: Typically October-November
  • Peak: December-January (positivity peaked the week of December 21 in the 2024-25 season)
  • Season end: March-April
  • Vaccination window: August-January (must vaccinate before or early in the season for protection)
  • Revenue implication: Q3 and Q4 are the dominant revenue quarters for RSV vaccine manufacturers; Q1 and Q2 are trough quarters

The post-COVID era saw a temporary disruption in RSV seasonality (2020-2021: summer/fall surges due to pandemic non-pharmaceutical interventions), but by 2023-2024 the traditional winter pattern had re-established.

1.6 At-Risk Populations

Population Risk Factor Severity Prevention Strategy
Adults 75+ Age-related immune decline High: highest hospitalization rate among adults Universal RSV vaccination (ACIP)
Adults 50-74 with comorbidities COPD, CHF, diabetes, chronic kidney/liver disease, severe obesity, immunocompromise Moderate-high Risk-based RSV vaccination (ACIP)
Immunocompromised adults Transplant, HIV, hematologic malignancy, immunosuppressive therapy High: prolonged viral shedding, higher mortality Vaccination (Abrysvo approved for 18-59 at risk)
Pregnant women (32-36 weeks) N/A (vaccine protects the infant) N/A Maternal Abrysvo vaccination OR infant monoclonal antibody
Infants (<6 months) Immature immune system, small airways Highest hospitalization risk of any age group Passive immunization: nirsevimab or clesrovimab; OR maternal vaccination
Premature infants Underdeveloped lungs, lack of maternal antibodies Very high Palivizumab (legacy), nirsevimab/clesrovimab
Children with BPD/CHD Chronic lung disease, congenital heart disease High Nirsevimab/clesrovimab; palivizumab for highest risk

2. Treatment Algorithm / Prevention Paradigm

2.1 The Fundamental Challenge: No Antiviral Treatment Exists

There is no approved antiviral treatment for RSV infection. Once a patient develops RSV disease, management is entirely supportive — supplemental oxygen, IV fluids, mechanical ventilation for severe cases. Ribavirin (aerosolized) has limited use in immunocompromised patients but is not standard of care due to marginal efficacy and significant toxicity.

This means prevention is the entire strategy for RSV. The market is structured around prophylaxis, not therapy — a fundamentally different dynamic than most infectious disease markets.

2.2 Prevention Modalities

The RSV prevention landscape uses two distinct immunization approaches:

Passive immunization (antibody-based): Direct administration of pre-formed antibodies that provide immediate but time-limited protection. No immune memory generated — protection wanes as antibodies are cleared.

Product Target Mechanism Duration Population
Palivizumab (Synagis) RSV F protein (site II) Humanized monoclonal antibody ~30 days per dose (monthly dosing required, 5 doses/season) High-risk infants only (premature, BPD, CHD)
Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) RSV preF protein (site zero) Long-acting monoclonal antibody ~5 months (single dose) All infants entering first RSV season
Clesrovimab (Enflonsia) RSV F protein (site IV, pre- and post-F) Long-acting monoclonal antibody ~5 months (single dose) All infants entering first RSV season (approved June 2025)

Active immunization (vaccine-based): Stimulates the recipient's immune system to generate antibodies and memory cells. Slower onset but potentially longer-lasting protection.

Product Target Platform Population
Abrysvo (Pfizer) RSV A + B preF protein Bivalent protein subunit Adults 60+, maternal (32-36 wks), adults 18-59 at risk
Arexvy (GSK) RSV A preF protein Adjuvanted (AS01E) protein subunit Adults 60+, adults 18-59 at risk
mResvia (Moderna) RSV preF protein mRNA Adults 60+, adults 18-59 at risk

2.3 The Maternal vs Direct Infant Protection Debate

This is a critical strategic dynamic for Abrysvo. Two pathways exist to protect infants from RSV:

Path 1 — Maternal vaccination (Abrysvo):

  • Vaccine administered to pregnant women at 32-36 weeks gestation
  • Maternal antibodies cross the placenta and provide passive protection to the newborn
  • Protection present at birth (no delay for infant immune response)
  • Efficacy: 81.8% against severe infant LRTD within 90 days; 69.4% within 180 days
  • Concern: numerical imbalance in preterm births in clinical trials (5.7% vaccine vs 4.7% placebo), leading FDA to restrict to 32-36 weeks gestation
  • Coverage: ~40% of eligible pregnant women vaccinated in the 2024-25 season

Path 2 — Direct infant immunization (nirsevimab/clesrovimab):

  • Monoclonal antibody given directly to the infant after birth or before RSV season
  • Does not depend on maternal immune response or timing of pregnancy
  • Nirsevimab: 77% protective against RSV hospitalization in 2024-25 real-world data
  • Clesrovimab: 84% reduction in RSV hospitalizations in clinical trials
  • No preterm birth concern (given postnatally)
  • Practical advantages: works regardless of timing of pregnancy; can be given to all infants including those whose mothers were not vaccinated

ACIP position: Both maternal vaccination and infant monoclonal antibody are recommended options. Most infants do not need both. In practice, when nirsevimab (or clesrovimab) is available, many providers and families prefer the direct infant immunization path because it avoids the preterm birth concern associated with maternal vaccination and does not require timing the vaccine to a narrow gestational window.

The maternal Abrysvo indication remains commercially relevant, particularly in settings where infant monoclonal antibody supply is constrained or where the pregnant patient prefers active vaccination. However, the competitive pressure from nirsevimab/clesrovimab has significantly limited the growth potential of Abrysvo's maternal indication in the US.

3. Product Profile: Abrysvo

3.1 Mechanism of Action

Abrysvo is a bivalent, prefusion F protein subunit vaccine containing stabilized preF antigens from both RSV subgroup A and RSV subgroup B. The vaccine works by:

  1. Presenting stabilized prefusion F protein to the immune system
  2. Eliciting neutralizing antibodies directed at antigenic site zero (preF-specific) and other conserved epitopes
  3. Generating both RSV A- and RSV B-specific neutralizing antibodies due to the bivalent formulation
  4. Inducing immunological memory for sustained protection

Key differentiator from competitors: Abrysvo is unadjuvanted — it relies solely on the immunogenicity of the prefusion F antigen without an adjuvant. This contrasts with Arexvy (GSK), which uses the AS01E adjuvant to boost immune response. The absence of an adjuvant may contribute to Abrysvo's favorable safety profile (potentially lower risk of inflammatory adverse events including GBS).

3.2 Clinical Data

RENOIR Trial (Adults 60+)

The pivotal Phase 3 RENOIR trial (NCT05035212) enrolled approximately 37,000 adults aged 60+ across multiple countries.

Season 1 efficacy:

  • 88.9% (95% CI: 53.6-98.7%) against RSV-LRTD with 3+ symptoms
  • 66.7% against RSV-LRTD with 2+ symptoms
  • 78.8% against severe RSV-LRTD

Season 2 efficacy (single dose, no revaccination):

  • 77.8% (95% CI: 51.4-91.1%) against RSV-LRTD with 3+ symptoms
  • Consistent efficacy maintained against both RSV A and RSV B subtypes
  • Modest decline from season 1 to season 2, but protection remained clinically meaningful

Safety profile:

  • Generally well-tolerated
  • Common adverse events: injection site pain, fatigue, headache, myalgia
  • GBS signal: FDA identified a potential risk and mandated labeling updates in January 2025 (similar to Arexvy). The estimated excess GBS risk is approximately 3 cases per million doses (lower than the ~7 excess cases per million reported for Arexvy)

MATISSE Trial (Maternal/Infant)

The Phase 3 MATISSE trial (NCT04424316) randomized ~7,400 pregnant women (32-36 weeks gestation) to Abrysvo or placebo.

Efficacy against severe infant LRTD:

  • 81.8% (99.5% CI: 40.6-96.3%) within 90 days of birth
  • 91.1% (95% CI: 38.8-99.8%) within 90 days (post-hoc analysis)
  • 69.4% within 180 days of birth

Efficacy against infant LRTD (any severity):

  • 34.7% (95% CI: -34.6 to 69.3%) within 90 days (not statistically significant)
  • 57.3% (95% CI: 29.8-74.7%) within 180 days

Safety — the preterm birth question:

  • Numerical imbalance in preterm births: 5.7% (Abrysvo) vs 4.7% (placebo) — a 1% absolute difference
  • Numerical imbalance in hypertensive disorders of pregnancy: 4.1% (Abrysvo) vs 3.3% (placebo)
  • FDA response: restricted the approved gestational window to 32-36 weeks to avoid very preterm/extremely preterm births
  • Post-authorization surveillance (Vaccine Safety Datalink, 2023-24 season): preterm birth incidence of 4.1% among vaccinated women, within the expected background range (3.1-6.1%)
  • FDA has required Pfizer to conduct additional post-marketing studies to further characterize this risk

Immunocompromised Adults Study

Pfizer reported positive results for Abrysvo in immunocompromised adults (transplant recipients, HIV, autoimmune diseases on immunosuppressants), supporting the 18-59 at-risk indication expansion. A single dose was immunogenic in this population, though absolute antibody titers were lower than in immunocompetent adults.

3.3 Approved Indications (as of April 2026)

Indication Approval Date Population Dosing ACIP Recommendation
Adults 60+ May 2023 Prevention of RSV-LRTD Single IM dose Universal for 75+; risk-based 50-74
Maternal August 2023 Prevention of infant RSV-LRTD via maternal vaccination Single IM dose at 32-36 weeks gestation (September-January) Recommended as alternative to infant monoclonal antibody
Adults 18-59 at increased risk 2024 (FDA); April 2025 (ACIP expansion to 50+) Prevention of RSV-LRTD in adults with chronic conditions Single IM dose Risk-based for 50-59 with conditions (COPD, CHF, diabetes, immunocompromise, etc.)

3.4 Dosing, Administration, and Storage

  • Dose: Single 0.5 mL intramuscular injection (deltoid)
  • Preparation: Lyophilized powder reconstituted with sterile water diluent
  • Storage: Refrigerated (2-8 degrees C); after reconstitution, administer within 4 hours
  • Coadministration: Can be given with seasonal influenza vaccine; evidence supports triple coadministration with COVID-19 and influenza vaccines with acceptable immunogenicity and safety profiles
  • No booster/revaccination recommendation at this time — see durability section below

3.5 The Durability Question

The single most important unresolved question for the adult RSV vaccine market: do patients need annual revaccination, or is one dose sufficient?

Current evidence:

  • RENOIR data shows efficacy of 77.8% in season 2 from a single dose (vs 88.9% in season 1) — modest waning but still protective
  • CDC currently recommends a single dose only for all indicated populations
  • No revaccination guidance has been issued
  • The "one and done" framing is a major commercial advantage (patients and HCPs prefer simplicity)
  • However, if durability data shows significant waning beyond season 2, annual revaccination could emerge — which would fundamentally change the revenue model from a one-time bolus to a recurring annual franchise

Pfizer's strategy appears to be emphasizing the single-dose durability message while generating longer-term follow-up data. A shift to annual dosing would be commercially favorable (recurring revenue) but could undermine the simplicity messaging that drives acceptance.

4. Competitive Landscape

4.1 Arexvy (GSK) — The Market Leader Under Pressure

Attribute Detail
Mechanism Adjuvanted (AS01E) monovalent prefusion F protein subunit
Approval May 2023 (adults 60+); expanded to 50-59 at risk (2025); 18-49 at risk (March 2026)
Adjuvant AS01E (same adjuvant system as Shingrix) — drives stronger immune response but also higher reactogenicity
Key efficacy AReSVi-006 trial: 82.6% efficacy against RSV-LRTD (3+ symptoms) in season 1; maintained efficacy through season 2

Revenue trajectory:

  • 2023: GBP 1.2 billion (~$1.5 billion) — blockbuster in year one, first-to-market advantage
  • 2024: GBP 590 million (~$740 million) — 50%+ decline from 2023; Q4 2024 sales down 69% YoY
  • 2025: Continued decline in US (-40% estimate); partial offset from international expansion and 50-59 label expansion

GBS safety signal:

  • FDA identified ~7 excess GBS cases per million Arexvy doses in adults 65+ (higher than the ~3 per million estimated for Abrysvo)
  • FDA mandated Warnings and Precautions labeling update in January 2025
  • FDA has not established a causal relationship but the signal is statistically notable
  • The AS01E adjuvant is suspected to contribute to the higher GBS signal (adjuvant-driven immune activation hypothesis)
  • Impact: some HCPs and pharmacists report increased hesitancy, particularly for patients with history of GBS or autoimmune conditions

Competitive position:

  • Still holds the largest market share (~two-thirds of US retail pharmacy RSV vaccine share in 2023-24)
  • Pharmacy formulary contracts and first-mover familiarity sustain market position despite efficacy and safety comparisons
  • The GBS signal is a strategic vulnerability that Pfizer has not aggressively exploited (likely due to its own, albeit smaller, GBS signal)
  • Real-world evidence from GSK shows Arexvy associated with reductions in heart attack, stroke, and severe COPD/asthma flare-ups — a potential differentiation message

4.2 mResvia (Moderna) — mRNA Platform, Commercial Failure

Attribute Detail
Mechanism mRNA-encoded prefusion F protein (mRNA-1345)
Approval May 2024 (adults 60+); expanded to 18-59 at risk
Key differentiator mRNA platform; pre-filled syringe format; NO GBS signal in clinical trials
Efficacy 83.7% against RSV-LRTD (3+ symptoms) in Phase 3

Revenue trajectory — catastrophic:

  • Q3 2024 (launch quarter): $10 million
  • Q3 2025: $2 million
  • Full-year 2025: Estimated <$25 million total
  • By far the weakest commercial launch of any RSV product

Why it failed commercially:

  1. Third to market: Launched 12 months after Arexvy and Abrysvo, by which time formulary contracts and HCP habits were established
  2. mRNA fatigue/hesitancy: Post-COVID negative sentiment toward mRNA technology among a subset of patients and HCPs
  3. No compelling differentiation story: The "no GBS signal" advantage was difficult to communicate given that GBS is rare for all RSV vaccines
  4. CDC recommendation narrowing: Market contraction happened simultaneously with mResvia's launch
  5. Pharmacy inertia: Pharmacies had already stocked and promoted Abrysvo/Arexvy; limited shelf space and formulary slots

Silver lining: mResvia reportedly achieved ~34% share in the 65+ segment by early 2026, largely due to its pre-filled syringe format, which pharmacists prefer for ease of administration. But this has not translated into meaningful absolute revenue.

Strategic implication: mResvia's failure suggests that in a category where HCPs make brand-agnostic recommendations (ZoomRx data: 8/10 HCPs don't name a brand), being third to market with marginal differentiation is commercially fatal — even with a potentially superior safety profile.

4.3 Nirsevimab / Beyfortus (Sanofi/AstraZeneca) — Infant Market Competitor

Attribute Detail
Mechanism Long-acting monoclonal antibody targeting RSV preF protein (site zero)
Approval July 2023 (infants <8 months entering first RSV season; children 8-19 months at risk for second season)
Administration Single IM injection; weight-based dosing (50 mg for <5 kg, 100 mg for 5+ kg)
Real-world efficacy 77% protective against RSV hospitalization in 2024-25 season (CDC study)

Impact on Abrysvo maternal market:

  • Nirsevimab competes directly with maternal Abrysvo for the "infant RSV protection" market
  • Many pediatricians and obstetricians prefer giving the monoclonal antibody directly to the infant rather than vaccinating the mother — it removes timing complexity and preterm birth concerns
  • ACIP does not formally prefer one approach over the other, but practical considerations favor direct infant immunization when supply allows
  • Supply challenges in the 2023-24 season initially limited nirsevimab availability, creating a window for maternal Abrysvo; supply has improved in subsequent seasons

4.4 Clesrovimab / Enflonsia (Merck) — New Entrant in Infant Space

Attribute Detail
Mechanism Long-acting monoclonal antibody targeting RSV F protein site IV (accessible on both pre-F and post-F)
Approval June 2025 (infants <8 months entering first RSV season)
Key differentiator Fixed dose (105 mg) regardless of weight — simpler than nirsevimab's weight-based dosing
Efficacy 84% reduction in RSV hospitalizations; 61% reduction in medically attended LRTI

Impact: Clesrovimab adds a second monoclonal antibody option for infant RSV prevention, further crowding the infant protection space and further marginalizing the maternal Abrysvo indication. ACIP considers nirsevimab and clesrovimab interchangeable, with selection based on availability, formulary, and provider preference.

4.5 Pipeline — Next-Generation RSV Approaches

Candidate Sponsor Platform Stage Key Feature
RSV/hMPV combination (IVX-A12) Icosavax/AstraZeneca VLP-based Phase 2 Dual pathogen coverage (RSV + hMPV); could become next-gen standard
SCB-1022/SCB-1033 Clover Bio Protein-based Phase 1 → Phase 2 (H1 2026) RSV + hMPV + PIV3 triple combination; first-in-class potential
MSD RSV vaccine Merck Undisclosed Phase 2/3 Merck mulling 2025/2026 launch after Phase 2/3 win
Co-administration studies Multiple Various Ongoing Triple co-admin of RSV + COVID-19 + influenza vaccines showing acceptable results

Strategic implication: The next competitive wave is combination vaccines (RSV + hMPV, RSV + flu + COVID). If a single shot can cover multiple respiratory pathogens, standalone RSV vaccines face commoditization risk. Pfizer's response may involve developing its own combination approach or emphasizing Abrysvo's established safety and efficacy data against emerging alternatives.

5. ACIP/CDC Dynamics — The Full Story

The ACIP recommendation trajectory for RSV vaccines is one of the most consequential regulatory stories in recent vaccine history. It fundamentally shaped the commercial trajectory of every player in the market.

5.1 Timeline of Key ACIP Decisions

June 2023 — Initial recommendation (broad but hesitant):

  • ACIP recommended RSV vaccination for adults 60+ using shared clinical decision-making (not a universal recommendation)
  • This was the first-ever ACIP recommendation for an RSV vaccine
  • "Shared clinical decision" meant HCPs and patients should discuss whether vaccination is appropriate — a weaker endorsement than universal recommendation
  • Impact: created ambiguity among HCPs about who should be vaccinated; vaccination rates were lower than expected for a new vaccine category

June 2024 — Narrowing that shook the market:

  • ACIP replaced the shared clinical decision for all 60+ with a split recommendation:
    • 75+ years: Universal recommendation (all adults should receive one dose)
    • 60-74 years: Shared clinical decision only for those with increased risk (chronic lung disease, chronic heart disease, immunocompromised, diabetes, severe obesity, residents of long-term care facilities, other conditions that increase risk)
  • This effectively narrowed the eligible population by removing healthy 60-74-year-olds from the recommendation
  • Impact: massive commercial disruption. All three RSV vaccine manufacturers saw US sales decline 40-70% in subsequent quarters

Why ACIP narrowed:

  1. Efficacy data did not demonstrate a clear benefit-risk ratio for healthy 60-64-year-olds (low baseline RSV hospitalization risk)
  2. GBS safety signal for both Arexvy and Abrysvo raised concerns about recommending a vaccine to a population with low absolute risk
  3. Cost-effectiveness analyses showed diminishing value below age 75

April 2025 — Partial expansion:

  • ACIP voted to expand the age-based recommendation downward:
    • Adults 50-59 years at increased risk now eligible for RSV vaccination
    • Risk-based criteria similar to 60-74 group (COPD, CHF, immunocompromise, etc.)
  • Both Abrysvo and Arexvy had received FDA approval for this age group
  • Impact: added ~15,000-20,000 hospitalization-risk patients/year to the eligible pool, but this is a risk-based (not universal) recommendation, limiting the population expansion

5.2 Current ACIP Recommendations (as of April 2026)

Age Group Recommendation Type
75+ years All adults should receive a single dose of RSV vaccine Universal
50-74 years with increased risk Adults with chronic conditions should receive a single dose Risk-based (shared clinical decision)
50-74 years healthy Not recommended N/A
18-49 years at increased risk FDA-approved but awaiting broader ACIP recommendation Limited
Pregnant women (32-36 weeks) Abrysvo recommended September-January; alternative to infant monoclonal antibody Shared decision

5.3 Impact on the Market

The ACIP dynamics have created several structural challenges:

  1. HCP confusion: ZoomRx conversation data shows HCPs citing inconsistent age thresholds (60, 65, 75) for RSV vaccination eligibility — the multiple recommendation changes have created persistent confusion
  2. Dampened demand: The total addressable adult RSV vaccine market in the US contracted ~50% between 2023 and 2024-25 due to the narrowed recommendations
  3. Asymmetric impact: Arexvy (first to market, larger installed base) suffered the largest absolute revenue decline; Abrysvo's international expansion partially offset US softness
  4. Vaccination rates remain low: Only ~38-42% of recommended adults had been vaccinated by end of 2024-25 season — significant room for growth within even the narrowed population

5.4 Maternal ACIP Dynamics

  • CDC recommends either maternal Abrysvo vaccination OR infant monoclonal antibody (nirsevimab/clesrovimab) — most infants should not receive both
  • In practice, availability and timing drive the choice: if nirsevimab/clesrovimab is available, many providers default to the infant immunization path
  • Maternal vaccination coverage reached ~40% among eligible pregnant women in 2024-25 — relatively strong for a new vaccine in pregnancy, but indicating that ~60% chose the infant antibody path or no protection

6. Commercial Dynamics

6.1 Revenue Trajectory

Abrysvo annual revenue:

Year Revenue (est.) Key Drivers
2023 (H2 only) ~$890 million Launch year; broad 60+ shared clinical decision
2024 ~$755 million (FY) US decline from narrowed ACIP; international launch ramp
2025 ~$1 billion (FY) Q4 +136% operational growth; international expansion; 18-59 at-risk label; market share gains vs Arexvy

Quarterly seasonality pattern:

  • Q1 (Jan-Mar): Trough — post-season, minimal new vaccinations
  • Q2 (Apr-Jun): Trough — off-season
  • Q3 (Jul-Sep): Ramp — pre-season stocking, early vaccinations begin August
  • Q4 (Oct-Dec): Peak — core RSV vaccination season, highest revenue quarter

This concentration means ~70-80% of annual revenue falls in Q3+Q4, creating significant quarter-to-quarter volatility.

6.2 Market Share Dynamics

Product 2023 US Share 2024-25 Trend Key Factor
Arexvy (GSK) ~65% Declining First-mover fading; GBS signal; US revenue -40%+
Abrysvo (Pfizer) ~35% Growing Gaining share from Arexvy; international growth; maternal indication
mResvia (Moderna) 0% (launched 2024) Negligible Failed launch; <$25M in 2025

Abrysvo is gaining share from Arexvy in the US, driven by:

  • Favorable head-to-head safety profile (lower estimated GBS rate)
  • Broader indication set (maternal + adult + 18-59 at risk)
  • International expansion (EU approval for adults and maternal)
  • Pharmacy-level competitive dynamics as contracts come up for renewal

6.3 Channel Dynamics — Pharmacy vs Physician Office

Adult RSV vaccination occurs predominantly through two channels:

Retail pharmacy (CVS, Walgreens, Rite Aid, independent):

  • Dominant channel for adult RSV vaccination (~60-70% of doses administered)
  • Pharmacist influence is critical: whichever product the pharmacy stocks is what the patient receives
  • Pre-filled syringe format is preferred by pharmacists (favors mResvia; Abrysvo requires reconstitution)
  • Formulary contracts and wholesale agreements drive product availability at the pharmacy level
  • Arexvy holds approximately two-thirds of retail pharmacy market share, largely due to first-to-market formulary contracts

Physician office:

  • Secondary channel (~30-40% of doses)
  • HCPs who stock vaccine tend to administer it during wellness visits — but many refer patients to pharmacy instead
  • ZoomRx data: 4/20 patients in pneumococcal conversations were referred to pharmacy rather than receiving in-office vaccination (similar dynamic expected for RSV)
  • In-office stocking is the strongest predictor of same-day vaccination

6.4 Pricing and Market Access

  • WAC price: ~$295 per dose for Abrysvo (comparable to Arexvy)
  • Medicare Part D: Covers adult vaccines at $0 cost to patient under the Inflation Reduction Act
  • Commercial insurance: Generally favorable post-ACIP recommendation; coverage for 50-59 risk-based may have more prior authorization friction
  • International pricing: Varies; UK contract win for Pfizer in 2025 was a notable competitive victory over GSK

6.5 International Expansion

Abrysvo has gained traction internationally:

  • EU: Approved for adults and maternal use; expanded in April 2025 to 18-59 at risk
  • UK: Pfizer won the national RSV vaccine contract over GSK
  • International growth was a major driver of the 136% Q4 2025 operational revenue increase
  • International maternal markets are particularly important where infant monoclonal antibody supply is less reliable than in the US

7. ZoomRx Research Insights

7.1 Abrysvo Older Adult HCP-Patient Conversations (n=10, Nov-Dec 2024)

Key findings from audio-recorded clinical conversations:

  1. Brand-agnostic recommendations dominate (8/10): HCPs rarely name Abrysvo or any specific RSV vaccine. They say "RSV vaccine" or "RSV shot." This is the #1 structural challenge for brand differentiation — the category is being commoditized at the point of care.

  2. Disease severity under-communicated (7/10): Most HCPs describe RSV as a bad cold or common respiratory illness. The life-threatening nature of RSV in older adults (6,000-10,000 deaths/year) is not conveyed. Patients therefore lack urgency to get vaccinated.

  3. Efficacy data not discussed with patients: HCPs focus on dosing ("one shot"), side effects ("sore arm, maybe a little tired"), and CDC recommendation. The 88.9% efficacy against severe LRTD — Abrysvo's strongest clinical talking point — is essentially invisible in patient conversations.

  4. "One and done" messaging resonates strongly: When HCPs frame RSV vaccination as a single lifetime dose (like the pneumococcal vaccine), patient acceptance is significantly higher. This "simplicity sells" insight mirrors the Prevnar 20 finding.

  5. Access and stocking drive prescribing, not preference: HCPs who stock an RSV vaccine recommend it. Those who don't stock it refer to pharmacy — where the patient receives whichever product the pharmacy carries. The HCP has minimal influence on brand selection.

  6. Patients don't ask about vaccine efficacy: Questions center on side effects ("will I feel sick?"), administration logistics, and insurance coverage. No patient in the sample asked about clinical trial data or comparative efficacy.

7.2 RSV PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) — Multiple Waves

Quantitative tracking of Abrysvo field force effectiveness:

  • Rep engagement tracked across awareness, message recall, impact on prescribing
  • Results used to optimize field force detailing strategy
  • Specific metrics available in PET wave reports (not reproduced here)

7.3 RSV NPT (Non-Personal Promotional Tracking) — Multiple Waves

Non-personal (digital, DTC, conference) channel effectiveness tracking for Abrysvo.

8. Unmet Needs and Strategic Questions

8.1 Annual Revaccination Question

The biggest open question in adult RSV prevention: should it be an annual shot (like influenza) or a one-time/infrequent shot (like pneumococcal)?

  • Current data supports a single dose for at least two RSV seasons (77.8% efficacy in season 2)
  • Season 3+ data is pending — if efficacy drops significantly, annual revaccination may become necessary
  • Commercial implications are enormous: annual dosing would transform the revenue model from a shrinking one-time bolus to a recurring franchise
  • HCP and patient preference strongly favors "one and done" — any shift to annual would need to overcome messaging inertia
  • The revaccination question also affects category positioning: is RSV vaccination more like the flu shot (annual ritual) or more like the shingles shot (once in a lifetime)?

8.2 Maternal Market Recovery

Abrysvo's maternal indication faces existential competitive pressure:

  • Two monoclonal antibodies (nirsevimab, clesrovimab) now available for direct infant protection
  • The preterm birth safety concern, while largely addressed by post-marketing data, remains a persistent narrative barrier
  • Maternal market recovery depends on: (a) supply constraints for infant monoclonal antibodies, (b) OB/GYN preference for vaccination during pregnancy as part of routine prenatal care, (c) international markets where infant mAb supply is less reliable
  • Strategic question: should Pfizer continue investing in the maternal indication or redirect resources to the growing adult 50+ opportunity?

8.3 Brand Differentiation in a Brand-Agnostic Category

ZoomRx data reveals the fundamental commercial challenge: 8/10 HCPs don't name a brand when recommending RSV vaccination. In a brand-agnostic category:

  • Traditional detailing and medical education have limited impact on brand-level prescribing
  • Pharmacy formulary positioning and stocking become the primary competitive levers
  • The "which RSV vaccine" decision is made by the pharmacy buyer, not the physician
  • Opportunities for differentiation: (a) safety messaging (lower GBS signal vs Arexvy), (b) dual-indication positioning (adult + maternal), (c) age expansion leadership (first to establish in 50-59), (d) co-administration data with flu + COVID-19

8.4 Pharmacy Channel Optimization

Given that 60-70% of adult RSV vaccinations occur in retail pharmacies:

  • How does pharmacy-level stocking affect brand share?
  • What drives pharmacist brand preference — price, format, formulary mandates, or clinical data?
  • Can Pfizer's reconstitution requirement (lyophilized powder vs mResvia's pre-filled syringe) be a barrier at the pharmacy level?
  • How does the "pharmacy referral drop-off" (documented in ZoomRx Prevnar data) affect RSV vaccination completion rates?

8.5 Disease Severity Communication Gap

The most actionable finding from ZoomRx research: RSV is positioned as a mild illness by 70% of HCPs in patient conversations, despite causing 6,000-10,000 adult deaths per year. Closing this gap could:

  • Increase vaccination urgency among eligible patients
  • Differentiate Abrysvo if disease severity messaging is linked to efficacy data (88.9% against severe LRTD)
  • Support the risk-based recommendation for 50-74-year-olds (patients with comorbidities need to understand their specific risk)

8.6 The 50-59 At-Risk Opportunity

The April 2025 ACIP expansion to adults 50-59 with risk factors represents a new growth vector:

  • ~15,000-20,000 RSV hospitalizations/year in this age group
  • Both Abrysvo and Arexvy are FDA-approved for this population
  • However, the risk-based recommendation (not universal) means HCPs must identify eligible patients — a friction point
  • Research question: how are HCPs identifying and counseling 50-59-year-old at-risk patients about RSV vaccination? Is the process systematic or opportunistic?

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

headings.text:
1. Disease Landscape
1.1 RSV Virology
Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a negative-sense, single-stranded RNA virus of the family Pneumoviridae. It is an enveloped virus approximately 120-300 nm in diameter with two major surface glycoproteins that are critical to its pathogenic cycle:

Fusion (F) protein: Mediates fusion of the viral envelope with the host cell membrane. Exists in two conformations: a metastable prefusion (preF) state (lollipop-shaped, present on the virus surface before cell contact) and a stable postfusion (postF) state (crutch-shaped, after membrane fusion is complete). The prefusion conformation is the primary target for modern vaccine development because it exposes neutralization-sensitive epitopes (particularly antigenic site zero) that are hidden once the protein shifts to its postfusion form.
Attachment (G) protein: Mediates initial viral attachment to host cell surface glycosaminoglycans. More variable between subtypes than the F protein.

1.2 RSV A vs RSV B Subtypes
RSV circulates as two antigenic subtypes, RSV A and RSV B, which co-circulate during each seasonal epidemic with one typically predominating in a given year:



Feature
RSV A
RSV B




G protein
Highly variable between subtypes (~47% amino acid identity)
Distinct antigenic profile


F protein
Highly conserved (>90% amino acid identity across subtypes)
Highly conserved


Virulence
Historically associated with greater disease severity in some studies
Comparable severity in recent data


Predominance
Alternates yearly; more frequently dominant in US seasons
Less frequently dominant


Vaccine relevance
Both subtypes must be covered for a complete vaccine
Abrysvo's bivalent design addresses both



The critical insight for vaccine design: because the F protein is >90% conserved between RSV A and RSV B, prefusion F-based vaccines (including Abrysvo) can provide cross-subtype protection. Abrysvo's bivalent formulation includes stabilized preF antigens from both RSV A and RSV B lineages, and the RENOIR trial confirmed consistent efficacy against both subtypes across two RSV seasons.
1.3 Pathogenesis
RSV infects the respiratory epithelium via the nasopharynx and spreads to the lower respiratory tract through cell-to-cell fusion (forming syncytia — multinucleated giant cells — hence the virus name). The disease process involves:

Viral entry: PreF protein undergoes a triggered conformational change, inserting its fusion peptide into the host cell membrane, forming a six-helix bundle that pulls viral and cell membranes together
Epithelial damage: Direct cytopathic effect on airway epithelial cells, causing necrosis, sloughing, and mucus plugging
Inflammatory response: Neutrophilic infiltration, cytokine release (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-alpha), airway edema and bronchospasm
Resolution or progression: In healthy adults, self-limited upper respiratory illness. In high-risk populations, progression to bronchiolitis (infants), pneumonia, respiratory failure, and death

RSV does not confer durable natural immunity — reinfection occurs throughout life, though disease severity generally decreases with repeated exposures (except in the elderly and immunocompromised, where waning immunity restores susceptibility to severe disease).
1.4 Epidemiology — US Burden
RSV is one of the most significant respiratory pathogens in the US, with disease burden concentrated at the extremes of age:
Overall (2024-2025 season):

190,000-350,000 hospitalizations annually
10,000-23,000 deaths annually
RSV epidemic period: November 9, 2024 through March 29, 2025
RSV positivity peaked at 11.0% during the week ending December 21, 2024

Older adults (60+):

60,000-160,000 hospitalizations/year among adults 65+
6,000-10,000 deaths/year among adults 65+
82.1% of RSV deaths occur in adults aged 65+
Highest hospitalization rates among adults 75+ (426.9 per 100,000)
Annual economic burden: $6.6 billion for adults 60+ ($2.9B direct medical, $3.7B indirect costs)
Only 4% of RSV cases in this population are hospitalized, but these account for 94% of direct medical costs

Infants and young children:

RSV is the leading cause of infant hospitalization in the US
~58,000-80,000 hospitalizations/year in children under 5
Among 3.7 million US births annually: ~592,700 RSV-associated LRTI cases, 48,499 hospitalizations, 144,599 ED visits
Annual cost: $1.6 billion (infants 0-3 months account for 43% of costs)
Mean hospitalization cost: ~$12,000 per admission

Adults 50-59 at increased risk:

15,000-20,000 RSV-associated hospitalizations annually in this age group
Newly eligible for vaccination following the April 2025 ACIP expansion

1.5 Seasonality
RSV follows a predictable seasonal pattern in the continental US:

Season onset: Typically October-November
Peak: December-January (positivity peaked the week of December 21 in the 2024-25 season)
Season end: March-April
Vaccination window: August-January (must vaccinate before or early in the season for protection)
Revenue implication: Q3 and Q4 are the dominant revenue quarters for RSV vaccine manufacturers; Q1 and Q2 are trough quarters

The post-COVID era saw a temporary disruption in RSV seasonality (2020-2021: summer/fall surges due to pandemic non-pharmaceutical interventions), but by 2023-2024 the traditional winter pattern had re-established.
1.6 At-Risk Populations



Population
Risk Factor
Severity
Prevention Strategy




Adults 75+
Age-related immune decline
High: highest hospitalization rate among adults
Universal RSV vaccination (ACIP)


Adults 50-74 with comorbidities
COPD, CHF, diabetes, chronic kidney/liver disease, severe obesity, immunocompromise
Moderate-high
Risk-based RSV vaccination (ACIP)


Immunocompromised adults
Transplant, HIV, hematologic malignancy, immunosuppressive therapy
High: prolonged viral shedding, higher mortality
Vaccination (Abrysvo approved for 18-59 at risk)


Pregnant women (32-36 weeks)
N/A (vaccine protects the infant)
N/A
Maternal Abrysvo vaccination OR infant monoclonal antibody


Infants (<6 months)
Immature immune system, small airways
Highest hospitalization risk of any age group
Passive immunization: nirsevimab or clesrovimab; OR maternal vaccination


Premature infants
Underdeveloped lungs, lack of maternal antibodies
Very high
Palivizumab (legacy), nirsevimab/clesrovimab


Children with BPD/CHD
Chronic lung disease, congenital heart disease
High
Nirsevimab/clesrovimab; palivizumab for highest risk




2. Treatment Algorithm / Prevention Paradigm
2.1 The Fundamental Challenge: No Antiviral Treatment Exists
There is no approved antiviral treatment for RSV infection. Once a patient develops RSV disease, management is entirely supportive — supplemental oxygen, IV fluids, mechanical ventilation for severe cases. Ribavirin (aerosolized) has limited use in immunocompromised patients but is not standard of care due to marginal efficacy and significant toxicity.
This means prevention is the entire strategy for RSV. The market is structured around prophylaxis, not therapy — a fundamentally different dynamic than most infectious disease markets.
2.2 Prevention Modalities
The RSV prevention landscape uses two distinct immunization approaches:
Passive immunization (antibody-based):
Direct administration of pre-formed antibodies that provide immediate but time-limited protection. No immune memory generated — protection wanes as antibodies are cleared.



Product
Target
Mechanism
Duration
Population




Palivizumab (Synagis)
RSV F protein (site II)
Humanized monoclonal antibody
~30 days per dose (monthly dosing required, 5 doses/season)
High-risk infants only (premature, BPD, CHD)


Nirsevimab (Beyfortus)
RSV preF protein (site zero)
Long-acting monoclonal antibody
~5 months (single dose)
All infants entering first RSV season


Clesrovimab (Enflonsia)
RSV F protein (site IV, pre- and post-F)
Long-acting monoclonal antibody
~5 months (single dose)
All infants entering first RSV season (approved June 2025)



Active immunization (vaccine-based):
Stimulates the recipient's immune system to generate antibodies and memory cells. Slower onset but potentially longer-lasting protection.



Product
Target
Platform
Population




Abrysvo (Pfizer)
RSV A + B preF protein
Bivalent protein subunit
Adults 60+, maternal (32-36 wks), adults 18-59 at risk


Arexvy (GSK)
RSV A preF protein
Adjuvanted (AS01E) protein subunit
Adults 60+, adults 18-59 at risk


mResvia (Moderna)
RSV preF protein
mRNA
Adults 60+, adults 18-59 at risk



2.3 The Maternal vs Direct Infant Protection Debate
This is a critical strategic dynamic for Abrysvo. Two pathways exist to protect infants from RSV:
Path 1 — Maternal vaccination (Abrysvo):

Vaccine administered to pregnant women at 32-36 weeks gestation
Maternal antibodies cross the placenta and provide passive protection to the newborn
Protection present at birth (no delay for infant immune response)
Efficacy: 81.8% against severe infant LRTD within 90 days; 69.4% within 180 days
Concern: numerical imbalance in preterm births in clinical trials (5.7% vaccine vs 4.7% placebo), leading FDA to restrict to 32-36 weeks gestation
Coverage: ~40% of eligible pregnant women vaccinated in the 2024-25 season

Path 2 — Direct infant immunization (nirsevimab/clesrovimab):

Monoclonal antibody given directly to the infant after birth or before RSV season
Does not depend on maternal immune response or timing of pregnancy
Nirsevimab: 77% protective against RSV hospitalization in 2024-25 real-world data
Clesrovimab: 84% reduction in RSV hospitalizations in clinical trials
No preterm birth concern (given postnatally)
Practical advantages: works regardless of timing of pregnancy; can be given to all infants including those whose mothers were not vaccinated

ACIP position: Both maternal vaccination and infant monoclonal antibody are recommended options. Most infants do not need both. In practice, when nirsevimab (or clesrovimab) is available, many providers and families prefer the direct infant immunization path because it avoids the preterm birth concern associated with maternal vaccination and does not require timing the vaccine to a narrow gestational window.
The maternal Abrysvo indication remains commercially relevant, particularly in settings where infant monoclonal antibody supply is constrained or where the pregnant patient prefers active vaccination. However, the competitive pressure from nirsevimab/clesrovimab has significantly limited the growth potential of Abrysvo's maternal indication in the US.

3. Product Profile: Abrysvo
3.1 Mechanism of Action
Abrysvo is a bivalent, prefusion F protein subunit vaccine containing stabilized preF antigens from both RSV subgroup A and RSV subgroup B. The vaccine works by:

Presenting stabilized prefusion F protein to the immune system
Eliciting neutralizing antibodies directed at antigenic site zero (preF-specific) and other conserved epitopes
Generating both RSV A- and RSV B-specific neutralizing antibodies due to the bivalent formulation
Inducing immunological memory for sustained protection

Key differentiator from competitors: Abrysvo is unadjuvanted — it relies solely on the immunogenicity of the prefusion F antigen without an adjuvant. This contrasts with Arexvy (GSK), which uses the AS01E adjuvant to boost immune response. The absence of an adjuvant may contribute to Abrysvo's favorable safety profile (potentially lower risk of inflammatory adverse events including GBS).
3.2 Clinical Data
RENOIR Trial (Adults 60+)
The pivotal Phase 3 RENOIR trial (NCT05035212) enrolled approximately 37,000 adults aged 60+ across multiple countries.
Season 1 efficacy:

88.9% (95% CI: 53.6-98.7%) against RSV-LRTD with 3+ symptoms
66.7% against RSV-LRTD with 2+ symptoms
78.8% against severe RSV-LRTD

Season 2 efficacy (single dose, no revaccination):

77.8% (95% CI: 51.4-91.1%) against RSV-LRTD with 3+ symptoms
Consistent efficacy maintained against both RSV A and RSV B subtypes
Modest decline from season 1 to season 2, but protection remained clinically meaningful

Safety profile:

Generally well-tolerated
Common adverse events: injection site pain, fatigue, headache, myalgia
GBS signal: FDA identified a potential risk and mandated labeling updates in January 2025 (similar to Arexvy). The estimated excess GBS risk is approximately 3 cases per million doses (lower than the ~7 excess cases per million reported for Arexvy)

MATISSE Trial (Maternal/Infant)
The Phase 3 MATISSE trial (NCT04424316) randomized ~7,400 pregnant women (32-36 weeks gestation) to Abrysvo or placebo.
Efficacy against severe infant LRTD:

81.8% (99.5% CI: 40.6-96.3%) within 90 days of birth
91.1% (95% CI: 38.8-99.8%) within 90 days (post-hoc analysis)
69.4% within 180 days of birth

Efficacy against infant LRTD (any severity):

34.7% (95% CI: -34.6 to 69.3%) within 90 days (not statistically significant)
57.3% (95% CI: 29.8-74.7%) within 180 days

Safety — the preterm birth question:

Numerical imbalance in preterm births: 5.7% (Abrysvo) vs 4.7% (placebo) — a 1% absolute difference
Numerical imbalance in hypertensive disorders of pregnancy: 4.1% (Abrysvo) vs 3.3% (placebo)
FDA response: restricted the approved gestational window to 32-36 weeks to avoid very preterm/extremely preterm births
Post-authorization surveillance (Vaccine Safety Datalink, 2023-24 season): preterm birth incidence of 4.1% among vaccinated women, within the expected background range (3.1-6.1%)
FDA has required Pfizer to conduct additional post-marketing studies to further characterize this risk

Immunocompromised Adults Study
Pfizer reported positive results for Abrysvo in immunocompromised adults (transplant recipients, HIV, autoimmune diseases on immunosuppressants), supporting the 18-59 at-risk indication expansion. A single dose was immunogenic in this population, though absolute antibody titers were lower than in immunocompetent adults.
3.3 Approved Indications (as of April 2026)



Indication
Approval Date
Population
Dosing
ACIP Recommendation




Adults 60+
May 2023
Prevention of RSV-LRTD
Single IM dose
Universal for 75+; risk-based 50-74


Maternal
August 2023
Prevention of infant RSV-LRTD via maternal vaccination
Single IM dose at 32-36 weeks gestation (September-January)
Recommended as alternative to infant monoclonal antibody


Adults 18-59 at increased risk
2024 (FDA); April 2025 (ACIP expansion to 50+)
Prevention of RSV-LRTD in adults with chronic conditions
Single IM dose
Risk-based for 50-59 with conditions (COPD, CHF, diabetes, immunocompromise, etc.)



3.4 Dosing, Administration, and Storage

Dose: Single 0.5 mL intramuscular injection (deltoid)
Preparation: Lyophilized powder reconstituted with sterile water diluent
Storage: Refrigerated (2-8 degrees C); after reconstitution, administer within 4 hours
Coadministration: Can be given with seasonal influenza vaccine; evidence supports triple coadministration with COVID-19 and influenza vaccines with acceptable immunogenicity and safety profiles
No booster/revaccination recommendation at this time — see durability section below

3.5 The Durability Question
The single most important unresolved question for the adult RSV vaccine market: do patients need annual revaccination, or is one dose sufficient?
Current evidence:

RENOIR data shows efficacy of 77.8% in season 2 from a single dose (vs 88.9% in season 1) — modest waning but still protective
CDC currently recommends a single dose only for all indicated populations
No revaccination guidance has been issued
The "one and done" framing is a major commercial advantage (patients and HCPs prefer simplicity)
However, if durability data shows significant waning beyond season 2, annual revaccination could emerge — which would fundamentally change the revenue model from a one-time bolus to a recurring annual franchise

Pfizer's strategy appears to be emphasizing the single-dose durability message while generating longer-term follow-up data. A shift to annual dosing would be commercially favorable (recurring revenue) but could undermine the simplicity messaging that drives acceptance.

4. Competitive Landscape
4.1 Arexvy (GSK) — The Market Leader Under Pressure



Attribute
Detail




Mechanism
Adjuvanted (AS01E) monovalent prefusion F protein subunit


Approval
May 2023 (adults 60+); expanded to 50-59 at risk (2025); 18-49 at risk (March 2026)


Adjuvant
AS01E (same adjuvant system as Shingrix) — drives stronger immune response but also higher reactogenicity


Key efficacy
AReSVi-006 trial: 82.6% efficacy against RSV-LRTD (3+ symptoms) in season 1; maintained efficacy through season 2



Revenue trajectory:

2023: GBP 1.2 billion (~$1.5 billion) — blockbuster in year one, first-to-market advantage
2024: GBP 590 million (~$740 million) — 50%+ decline from 2023; Q4 2024 sales down 69% YoY
2025: Continued decline in US (-40% estimate); partial offset from international expansion and 50-59 label expansion

GBS safety signal:

FDA identified ~7 excess GBS cases per million Arexvy doses in adults 65+ (higher than the ~3 per million estimated for Abrysvo)
FDA mandated Warnings and Precautions labeling update in January 2025
FDA has not established a causal relationship but the signal is statistically notable
The AS01E adjuvant is suspected to contribute to the higher GBS signal (adjuvant-driven immune activation hypothesis)
Impact: some HCPs and pharmacists report increased hesitancy, particularly for patients with history of GBS or autoimmune conditions

Competitive position:

Still holds the largest market share (~two-thirds of US retail pharmacy RSV vaccine share in 2023-24)
Pharmacy formulary contracts and first-mover familiarity sustain market position despite efficacy and safety comparisons
The GBS signal is a strategic vulnerability that Pfizer has not aggressively exploited (likely due to its own, albeit smaller, GBS signal)
Real-world evidence from GSK shows Arexvy associated with reductions in heart attack, stroke, and severe COPD/asthma flare-ups — a potential differentiation message

4.2 mResvia (Moderna) — mRNA Platform, Commercial Failure



Attribute
Detail




Mechanism
mRNA-encoded prefusion F protein (mRNA-1345)


Approval
May 2024 (adults 60+); expanded to 18-59 at risk


Key differentiator
mRNA platform; pre-filled syringe format; NO GBS signal in clinical trials


Efficacy
83.7% against RSV-LRTD (3+ symptoms) in Phase 3



Revenue trajectory — catastrophic:

Q3 2024 (launch quarter): $10 million
Q3 2025: $2 million
Full-year 2025: Estimated <$25 million total
By far the weakest commercial launch of any RSV product

Why it failed commercially:

Third to market: Launched 12 months after Arexvy and Abrysvo, by which time formulary contracts and HCP habits were established
mRNA fatigue/hesitancy: Post-COVID negative sentiment toward mRNA technology among a subset of patients and HCPs
No compelling differentiation story: The "no GBS signal" advantage was difficult to communicate given that GBS is rare for all RSV vaccines
CDC recommendation narrowing: Market contraction happened simultaneously with mResvia's launch
Pharmacy inertia: Pharmacies had already stocked and promoted Abrysvo/Arexvy; limited shelf space and formulary slots

Silver lining: mResvia reportedly achieved ~34% share in the 65+ segment by early 2026, largely due to its pre-filled syringe format, which pharmacists prefer for ease of administration. But this has not translated into meaningful absolute revenue.
Strategic implication: mResvia's failure suggests that in a category where HCPs make brand-agnostic recommendations (ZoomRx data: 8/10 HCPs don't name a brand), being third to market with marginal differentiation is commercially fatal — even with a potentially superior safety profile.
4.3 Nirsevimab / Beyfortus (Sanofi/AstraZeneca) — Infant Market Competitor



Attribute
Detail




Mechanism
Long-acting monoclonal antibody targeting RSV preF protein (site zero)


Approval
July 2023 (infants <8 months entering first RSV season; children 8-19 months at risk for second season)


Administration
Single IM injection; weight-based dosing (50 mg for <5 kg, 100 mg for 5+ kg)


Real-world efficacy
77% protective against RSV hospitalization in 2024-25 season (CDC study)



Impact on Abrysvo maternal market:

Nirsevimab competes directly with maternal Abrysvo for the "infant RSV protection" market
Many pediatricians and obstetricians prefer giving the monoclonal antibody directly to the infant rather than vaccinating the mother — it removes timing complexity and preterm birth concerns
ACIP does not formally prefer one approach over the other, but practical considerations favor direct infant immunization when supply allows
Supply challenges in the 2023-24 season initially limited nirsevimab availability, creating a window for maternal Abrysvo; supply has improved in subsequent seasons

4.4 Clesrovimab / Enflonsia (Merck) — New Entrant in Infant Space



Attribute
Detail




Mechanism
Long-acting monoclonal antibody targeting RSV F protein site IV (accessible on both pre-F and post-F)


Approval
June 2025 (infants <8 months entering first RSV season)


Key differentiator
Fixed dose (105 mg) regardless of weight — simpler than nirsevimab's weight-based dosing


Efficacy
84% reduction in RSV hospitalizations; 61% reduction in medically attended LRTI



Impact: Clesrovimab adds a second monoclonal antibody option for infant RSV prevention, further crowding the infant protection space and further marginalizing the maternal Abrysvo indication. ACIP considers nirsevimab and clesrovimab interchangeable, with selection based on availability, formulary, and provider preference.
4.5 Pipeline — Next-Generation RSV Approaches



Candidate
Sponsor
Platform
Stage
Key Feature




RSV/hMPV combination (IVX-A12)
Icosavax/AstraZeneca
VLP-based
Phase 2
Dual pathogen coverage (RSV + hMPV); could become next-gen standard


SCB-1022/SCB-1033
Clover Bio
Protein-based
Phase 1 → Phase 2 (H1 2026)
RSV + hMPV + PIV3 triple combination; first-in-class potential


MSD RSV vaccine
Merck
Undisclosed
Phase 2/3
Merck mulling 2025/2026 launch after Phase 2/3 win


Co-administration studies
Multiple
Various
Ongoing
Triple co-admin of RSV + COVID-19 + influenza vaccines showing acceptable results



Strategic implication: The next competitive wave is combination vaccines (RSV + hMPV, RSV + flu + COVID). If a single shot can cover multiple respiratory pathogens, standalone RSV vaccines face commoditization risk. Pfizer's response may involve developing its own combination approach or emphasizing Abrysvo's established safety and efficacy data against emerging alternatives.

5. ACIP/CDC Dynamics — The Full Story
The ACIP recommendation trajectory for RSV vaccines is one of the most consequential regulatory stories in recent vaccine history. It fundamentally shaped the commercial trajectory of every player in the market.
5.1 Timeline of Key ACIP Decisions
June 2023 — Initial recommendation (broad but hesitant):

ACIP recommended RSV vaccination for adults 60+ using shared clinical decision-making (not a universal recommendation)
This was the first-ever ACIP recommendation for an RSV vaccine
"Shared clinical decision" meant HCPs and patients should discuss whether vaccination is appropriate — a weaker endorsement than universal recommendation
Impact: created ambiguity among HCPs about who should be vaccinated; vaccination rates were lower than expected for a new vaccine category

June 2024 — Narrowing that shook the market:

ACIP replaced the shared clinical decision for all 60+ with a split recommendation:

75+ years: Universal recommendation (all adults should receive one dose)
60-74 years: Shared clinical decision only for those with increased risk (chronic lung disease, chronic heart disease, immunocompromised, diabetes, severe obesity, residents of long-term care facilities, other conditions that increase risk)


This effectively narrowed the eligible population by removing healthy 60-74-year-olds from the recommendation
Impact: massive commercial disruption. All three RSV vaccine manufacturers saw US sales decline 40-70% in subsequent quarters

Why ACIP narrowed:

Efficacy data did not demonstrate a clear benefit-risk ratio for healthy 60-64-year-olds (low baseline RSV hospitalization risk)
GBS safety signal for both Arexvy and Abrysvo raised concerns about recommending a vaccine to a population with low absolute risk
Cost-effectiveness analyses showed diminishing value below age 75

April 2025 — Partial expansion:

ACIP voted to expand the age-based recommendation downward:

Adults 50-59 years at increased risk now eligible for RSV vaccination
Risk-based criteria similar to 60-74 group (COPD, CHF, immunocompromise, etc.)


Both Abrysvo and Arexvy had received FDA approval for this age group
Impact: added ~15,000-20,000 hospitalization-risk patients/year to the eligible pool, but this is a risk-based (not universal) recommendation, limiting the population expansion

5.2 Current ACIP Recommendations (as of April 2026)



Age Group
Recommendation
Type




75+ years
All adults should receive a single dose of RSV vaccine
Universal


50-74 years with increased risk
Adults with chronic conditions should receive a single dose
Risk-based (shared clinical decision)


50-74 years healthy
Not recommended
N/A


18-49 years at increased risk
FDA-approved but awaiting broader ACIP recommendation
Limited


Pregnant women (32-36 weeks)
Abrysvo recommended September-January; alternative to infant monoclonal antibody
Shared decision



5.3 Impact on the Market
The ACIP dynamics have created several structural challenges:

HCP confusion: ZoomRx conversation data shows HCPs citing inconsistent age thresholds (60, 65, 75) for RSV vaccination eligibility — the multiple recommendation changes have created persistent confusion
Dampened demand: The total addressable adult RSV vaccine market in the US contracted ~50% between 2023 and 2024-25 due to the narrowed recommendations
Asymmetric impact: Arexvy (first to market, larger installed base) suffered the largest absolute revenue decline; Abrysvo's international expansion partially offset US softness
Vaccination rates remain low: Only ~38-42% of recommended adults had been vaccinated by end of 2024-25 season — significant room for growth within even the narrowed population

5.4 Maternal ACIP Dynamics

CDC recommends either maternal Abrysvo vaccination OR infant monoclonal antibody (nirsevimab/clesrovimab) — most infants should not receive both
In practice, availability and timing drive the choice: if nirsevimab/clesrovimab is available, many providers default to the infant immunization path
Maternal vaccination coverage reached ~40% among eligible pregnant women in 2024-25 — relatively strong for a new vaccine in pregnancy, but indicating that ~60% chose the infant antibody path or no protection


6. Commercial Dynamics
6.1 Revenue Trajectory
Abrysvo annual revenue:



Year
Revenue (est.)
Key Drivers




2023 (H2 only)
~$890 million
Launch year; broad 60+ shared clinical decision


2024
~$755 million (FY)
US decline from narrowed ACIP; international launch ramp


2025
~$1 billion (FY)
Q4 +136% operational growth; international expansion; 18-59 at-risk label; market share gains vs Arexvy



Quarterly seasonality pattern:

Q1 (Jan-Mar): Trough — post-season, minimal new vaccinations
Q2 (Apr-Jun): Trough — off-season
Q3 (Jul-Sep): Ramp — pre-season stocking, early vaccinations begin August
Q4 (Oct-Dec): Peak — core RSV vaccination season, highest revenue quarter

This concentration means ~70-80% of annual revenue falls in Q3+Q4, creating significant quarter-to-quarter volatility.
6.2 Market Share Dynamics



Product
2023 US Share
2024-25 Trend
Key Factor




Arexvy (GSK)
~65%
Declining
First-mover fading; GBS signal; US revenue -40%+


Abrysvo (Pfizer)
~35%
Growing
Gaining share from Arexvy; international growth; maternal indication


mResvia (Moderna)
0% (launched 2024)
Negligible
Failed launch; <$25M in 2025



Abrysvo is gaining share from Arexvy in the US, driven by:

Favorable head-to-head safety profile (lower estimated GBS rate)
Broader indication set (maternal + adult + 18-59 at risk)
International expansion (EU approval for adults and maternal)
Pharmacy-level competitive dynamics as contracts come up for renewal

6.3 Channel Dynamics — Pharmacy vs Physician Office
Adult RSV vaccination occurs predominantly through two channels:
Retail pharmacy (CVS, Walgreens, Rite Aid, independent):

Dominant channel for adult RSV vaccination (~60-70% of doses administered)
Pharmacist influence is critical: whichever product the pharmacy stocks is what the patient receives
Pre-filled syringe format is preferred by pharmacists (favors mResvia; Abrysvo requires reconstitution)
Formulary contracts and wholesale agreements drive product availability at the pharmacy level
Arexvy holds approximately two-thirds of retail pharmacy market share, largely due to first-to-market formulary contracts

Physician office:

Secondary channel (~30-40% of doses)
HCPs who stock vaccine tend to administer it during wellness visits — but many refer patients to pharmacy instead
ZoomRx data: 4/20 patients in pneumococcal conversations were referred to pharmacy rather than receiving in-office vaccination (similar dynamic expected for RSV)
In-office stocking is the strongest predictor of same-day vaccination

6.4 Pricing and Market Access

WAC price: ~$295 per dose for Abrysvo (comparable to Arexvy)
Medicare Part D: Covers adult vaccines at $0 cost to patient under the Inflation Reduction Act
Commercial insurance: Generally favorable post-ACIP recommendation; coverage for 50-59 risk-based may have more prior authorization friction
International pricing: Varies; UK contract win for Pfizer in 2025 was a notable competitive victory over GSK

6.5 International Expansion
Abrysvo has gained traction internationally:

EU: Approved for adults and maternal use; expanded in April 2025 to 18-59 at risk
UK: Pfizer won the national RSV vaccine contract over GSK
International growth was a major driver of the 136% Q4 2025 operational revenue increase
International maternal markets are particularly important where infant monoclonal antibody supply is less reliable than in the US


7. ZoomRx Research Insights
7.1 Abrysvo Older Adult HCP-Patient Conversations (n=10, Nov-Dec 2024)
Key findings from audio-recorded clinical conversations:


Brand-agnostic recommendations dominate (8/10): HCPs rarely name Abrysvo or any specific RSV vaccine. They say "RSV vaccine" or "RSV shot." This is the #1 structural challenge for brand differentiation — the category is being commoditized at the point of care.


Disease severity under-communicated (7/10): Most HCPs describe RSV as a bad cold or common respiratory illness. The life-threatening nature of RSV in older adults (6,000-10,000 deaths/year) is not conveyed. Patients therefore lack urgency to get vaccinated.


Efficacy data not discussed with patients: HCPs focus on dosing ("one shot"), side effects ("sore arm, maybe a little tired"), and CDC recommendation. The 88.9% efficacy against severe LRTD — Abrysvo's strongest clinical talking point — is essentially invisible in patient conversations.


"One and done" messaging resonates strongly: When HCPs frame RSV vaccination as a single lifetime dose (like the pneumococcal vaccine), patient acceptance is significantly higher. This "simplicity sells" insight mirrors the Prevnar 20 finding.


Access and stocking drive prescribing, not preference: HCPs who stock an RSV vaccine recommend it. Those who don't stock it refer to pharmacy — where the patient receives whichever product the pharmacy carries. The HCP has minimal influence on brand selection.


Patients don't ask about vaccine efficacy: Questions center on side effects ("will I feel sick?"), administration logistics, and insurance coverage. No patient in the sample asked about clinical trial data or comparative efficacy.


7.2 RSV PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) — Multiple Waves
Quantitative tracking of Abrysvo field force effectiveness:

Rep engagement tracked across awareness, message recall, impact on prescribing
Results used to optimize field force detailing strategy
Specific metrics available in PET wave reports (not reproduced here)

7.3 RSV NPT (Non-Personal Promotional Tracking) — Multiple Waves
Non-personal (digital, DTC, conference) channel effectiveness tracking for Abrysvo.

8. Unmet Needs and Strategic Questions
8.1 Annual Revaccination Question
The biggest open question in adult RSV prevention: should it be an annual shot (like influenza) or a one-time/infrequent shot (like pneumococcal)?

Current data supports a single dose for at least two RSV seasons (77.8% efficacy in season 2)
Season 3+ data is pending — if efficacy drops significantly, annual revaccination may become necessary
Commercial implications are enormous: annual dosing would transform the revenue model from a shrinking one-time bolus to a recurring franchise
HCP and patient preference strongly favors "one and done" — any shift to annual would need to overcome messaging inertia
The revaccination question also affects category positioning: is RSV vaccination more like the flu shot (annual ritual) or more like the shingles shot (once in a lifetime)?

8.2 Maternal Market Recovery
Abrysvo's maternal indication faces existential competitive pressure:

Two monoclonal antibodies (nirsevimab, clesrovimab) now available for direct infant protection
The preterm birth safety concern, while largely addressed by post-marketing data, remains a persistent narrative barrier
Maternal market recovery depends on: (a) supply constraints for infant monoclonal antibodies, (b) OB/GYN preference for vaccination during pregnancy as part of routine prenatal care, (c) international markets where infant mAb supply is less reliable
Strategic question: should Pfizer continue investing in the maternal indication or redirect resources to the growing adult 50+ opportunity?

8.3 Brand Differentiation in a Brand-Agnostic Category
ZoomRx data reveals the fundamental commercial challenge: 8/10 HCPs don't name a brand when recommending RSV vaccination. In a brand-agnostic category:

Traditional detailing and medical education have limited impact on brand-level prescribing
Pharmacy formulary positioning and stocking become the primary competitive levers
The "which RSV vaccine" decision is made by the pharmacy buyer, not the physician
Opportunities for differentiation: (a) safety messaging (lower GBS signal vs Arexvy), (b) dual-indication positioning (adult + maternal), (c) age expansion leadership (first to establish in 50-59), (d) co-administration data with flu + COVID-19

8.4 Pharmacy Channel Optimization
Given that 60-70% of adult RSV vaccinations occur in retail pharmacies:

How does pharmacy-level stocking affect brand share?
What drives pharmacist brand preference — price, format, formulary mandates, or clinical data?
Can Pfizer's reconstitution requirement (lyophilized powder vs mResvia's pre-filled syringe) be a barrier at the pharmacy level?
How does the "pharmacy referral drop-off" (documented in ZoomRx Prevnar data) affect RSV vaccination completion rates?

8.5 Disease Severity Communication Gap
The most actionable finding from ZoomRx research: RSV is positioned as a mild illness by 70% of HCPs in patient conversations, despite causing 6,000-10,000 adult deaths per year. Closing this gap could:

Increase vaccination urgency among eligible patients
Differentiate Abrysvo if disease severity messaging is linked to efficacy data (88.9% against severe LRTD)
Support the risk-based recommendation for 50-74-year-olds (patients with comorbidities need to understand their specific risk)

8.6 The 50-59 At-Risk Opportunity
